coredata.dc:title: Development of FRET-based fluorescence quenching of ferric ions using Gd-doped CeO2 quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Ferric ions (Fe3+) are important in transporting oxygen and enzymatic reactions. Excess (hyperferremia) or deficiency (hypoferremia) of Fe3+ can cause serious health problems such as injury to the kidney, hepatic cirrhosis, heart disease, and anemia. Therefore, a novel sensor with selective, sensitive, and quick determination abilities is urgently required for Fe3+ sensing. The groundbreaking nano-methodologies have uncovered the paramount importance of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles (MONPs) in facilitating more accurate and streamlined detection assays. Herein, gadolinium (Gd) doped and undoped fluorescent cerium oxide (CeO2) QDs were synthesized due to the luminescent property of cerium oxide and its ability to incorporate oxygen vacancies through doping by a facile solution method and applied for sensing Fe3+. The chemical and morphological properties of the doped and undoped CeO2 QDs were thoroughly characterized. Their electronic and optical properties were analyzed through UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, after which they have been utilized to detect Fe3+. The fluorescence emission spectrum of CeO2 QDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of ferric ions, resulting in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and subsequent quenching of the fluorescence emission. The newly synthesized sensor displayed impressive performance in terms of selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ sensing with a very low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.039 fM.

head.ce:title: Development of FRET-based fluorescence quenching of ferric ions using Gd-doped CeO2 quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferric ions (Fe3+) are important in transporting oxygen and enzymatic reactions. Excess (hyperferremia) or deficiency (hypoferremia) of Fe3+ can cause serious health problems such as injury to the kidney, hepatic cirrhosis, heart disease, and anemia. Therefore, a novel sensor with selective, sensitive, and quick determination abilities is urgently required for Fe3+ sensing. The groundbreaking nano-methodologies have uncovered the paramount importance of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles (MONPs) in facilitating more accurate and streamlined detection assays. Herein, gadolinium (Gd) doped and undoped fluorescent cerium oxide (CeO2) QDs were synthesized due to the luminescent property of cerium oxide and its ability to incorporate oxygen vacancies through doping by a facile solution method and applied for sensing Fe3+. The chemical and morphological properties of the doped and undoped CeO2 QDs were thoroughly characterized. Their electronic and optical properties were analyzed through UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, after which they have been utilized to detect Fe3+. The fluorescence emission spectrum of CeO2 QDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of ferric ions, resulting in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and subsequent quenching of the fluorescence emission. The newly synthesized sensor displayed impressive performance in terms of selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ sensing with a very low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.039 fM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ion (Fe3+) is a ubiquitous metal ion in biological and environmental systems, often complexed with variety of regulatory proteins and plays a key role in several physiological and pathological processes of the life system. It is used for providing energy, gene expression, neurotransmission and regulation of metalloenzymes [1]. The presence of an excess amount of iron (hyperferremia) in living cells may promote the oxidation of proteins, lipids, and other cellular components leading to the impairment of body organs. Cellular toxicity leads to serious diseases such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's diseases, neuro-inflammation, Huntingtin's disease, hepatitis, a decline of intelligence, agitans paralysis, renal failure, cancers, vomiting, diarrhea, and intestinal damage [2–4]. Also, the deficiency of Fe3+ in the human body is called hypoferremia which leads to anemia, low BP, decreased immunity, diabetes, liver, kidney, and heart damage [5].

According to the United States Environmental Protection Agency, the maximum allowable level of Fe3+ in drinking water is 0.3 ppm [6]. Therefore, developing a simple, sensitive, and selective sensing system for determining Fe3+ in clinical, medical, industrial, and environmental samples is much needed. Spectrometric techniques, electrochemical methods, total reflection X-ray fluorimetry, capillary electrophoresis, and flow injection analysis were employed traditionally for the detection of Fe3+. Even though these methods offer good analytical characteristics, there are numerous drawbacks such as complicated sample pre-treatment procedures, interference by other metal ions, expensive equipment, complicated instrumentation and operational procedures, time-consuming, labor-intensive, and difficulty in monitoring biological systems [7]. Thus, keen interest has been put forward by researchers in bringing up feasible modern technologies for favorable analysis.

Optical methods such as UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy have proved to be the most frequently used as well as the most favorable methods of analysis for recognizing environmental and biologically important analytes. This is due to their distinctive properties like non-invasive nature, low-cost, high sensitivity, fast analysis, operational simplicity, rapid responses and cost-effectiveness [2,5,8–10]. The progression in developing fluorescent molecular sensors for detecting Fe3+ is astounding. Information on the presence of ions in a sample is converted to quantifiable fluorescent signals, making this technique entice worldwide attention. To date, various molecules and materials like small organic dyes, conjugated polymers, nanomaterials, QDs, nanoclusters, up-conversion nanoparticles, DNAzymes, metal-organic frameworks, co-polymers, thin films, functionalized particles, and supramolecular complexes have been explored towards designing optical sensors [11–15].

Recently, nanotechnology especially QDs-driven technology has been evolving in the application of Fe3+ sensing. QDs have been in the limelight owing to their unique size-dependent change in optical, electronic, and electrical properties, arising from their quantum confinement effect [16]. Inorganic QDs have unique optical properties such as sharp emission peak, broad absorption peak, photostable, bright luminescence, long lifetime, a wide range of excitations and large Stokes shift [17]. A variety of QDs have been already utilized as promising chemical sensors. By conducting an extensive literature survey, it was found that Aleem et. al. synthesized a series of Eu3+ induced aggregates of water-soluble polysaccharide of 4-7 nm in size, which when excited at a wavelength of 400 nm emits bright red colored emission at 612 nm. This was used for the sensing Fe3+ through the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) mechanism to achieve a detection range of 1.0 × 10−5 - 1.0 × 10−1 M with LOD less than 1 ppm [18]. Sohal et. al. prepared spherically shaped citric acid-MnO2 QDs with an average size of 3.4 nm for the detection of Fe3+ via a dynamic quenching mechanism. The excitation wavelength was set to 300 nm to obtain a maximum emission intensity around 425 nm. The LOD was obtained to be 43 nM with a detection range of 0-10 µM [19]. Peng and Zhao synthesized silicon QDs with green emission at 515 nm under UV irradiation for highly selective Fe3+ sensing. The detection range was found to be 2-1000 µM with a LOD of 0.086 µM. The system was found to follow static quenching mechanism for sensing Fe3+ [20]. Kuang et. al. prepared a hydrophilic ratiometric Fe3+ fluorescence sensor with a detection limit of 26.5 nM in the range of 0-3.25 µM by integrating silica-embedded CdTe QDs with rhodamine-derivative. The sensor exhibited an emission peak at 635 nm with an excitation wavelength of 470 nm [21]. Lin et. al. synthesized blue-emitting polydopamine dots that could sense Fe3+ through its coordination with catechol groups. The mechanism involved oxidative photoinduced electron transfer to obtain a LOD of 0.3 µM with a linear range from 1 to 50 µM. Polydopamine dots were excited at 330 nm to obtain maximum emission at 440 nm [22]. A large number of biogenic carbon QDs have also been reported for fluorescence sensing [23], whereas the rare-earth metal oxide QDs offers several advantages as fluorescent sensor probes compared to that of biogenic carbon QDs. Rare-earth metal oxide QDs have higher luminescent properties with high quantum yields (QY) and stability. Their emission wavelength and intensity can be tuned through various methods such as doping, heating, combining with various elements, materials etc. They are highly resistant to photo-bleaching and less prone to environmental factors such as UV exposure, pH changes compared to carbonaceous materials. Further, the previous studies infer that not much work has been carried out in the area of inorganic, especially rare-earth metal oxide QDs for Fe3+ sensing and this gap urged us to explore the opportunities, by studying inorganic QDs.

Luminescent properties in rare-earth metal oxides such as cerium oxide (CeO2) nanoparticles mainly originate due to their 4f electronic shell. These materials are fascinating for numerous applications like fuel/solar cells, optical films, protective coatings and biomedical engineering owing to their favorable characteristics encompassing dual oxidation states, stability, and non-toxicity [24]. Among the different rare-earth metal oxides, CeO2 is regarded as one of the proficient fluorophores due to its inherent ability to generate oxygen vacancies (Ov), by extracting oxygen from the lattice of CeO2 [25]. The fluorescence characteristics of CeO2 could further be enhanced by judiciously adding dopants into the crystal lattice of CeO2. These dopants could induce some defects [26], which in turn could trigger its properties such as emission wavelength, QY and lifetime, etc. by reducing the energy barriers [27,28]. Especially, the trivalent dopants facilitate towards decreasing the lattice parameters and thus resulting in increased conversion of Ce4+ to Ce3+ ions and alike increase of Ov. Among the different rare earth elements, Gd3+ possesses the largest electron spin magnetic moment and maximum unpaired electrons which enables it to be used as an appropriate dopant for tuning the electrochemical and optical properties of different materials [29,30].

Thus, in this work, we envisage utilizing cerium oxide QDs synthesized using a modified sol-gel method [31] and doping it with gadolinium ions. Gd-(CeO2) was further applied as an efficient sensor for detecting Fe3+ through a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based dynamic mechanistic turn-off fluorescence sensing strategy. The sensor proved to be highly selective and sensitive, and demonstrated excellent anti-interference properties towards possible interferents. Finally, the sensor was employed to analyze real samples.

Cerium nitrate hexahydrate, urea, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), nitric acid (69–71 %) and gadolinium sulphate were bought from S D Fine-Chem Limited. Hydrogen peroxide (30 %) and Whatman filter paper were procured from Merck Chemicals, India. Ammonia solution (28-30 %) and anhydrous ethanol were obtained from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. All solutions were prepared in deionized (DI) water.

PXRD (Powder X-ray diffraction) pattern was recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance Panalytical X Pert3 XRD with Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ= 1.5406 Å). UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a JASCO V-670 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence studies were carried out on Hitachi F-7000 Spectrofluorometer with excitation and emission slit width of 5 nm and scan rate of 1200 nm/min. FT-IR spectra was recorded on Shimadzu IR Affinity-1. Transmission electron microscopy and elemental mapping for morphological characterization were captured on a TECHNAI G2-20 TWIN (FEI, USA) with an operating voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectroscopy was carried out on Anton Paar Cora 5001 DUAL. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained from ULVAC-PHI VersaSensor 4. Fluorescence lifetime was measured using JY Fluorolog-3-11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CeO2 and Gd-CeO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Undoped CeO2 QDs and Gd-doped CeO2 QDs were synthesized using sol-gel method as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Different molar ratios of gadolinium sulphate (0.025, 0.050, 0.075, 0.100) and cerium nitrate (0.975, 0.950, 0.925, 0.900) were taken such that the total concentration in the beaker remains 1 M in 20 mL of DI water. After stirring exuberantly for 15 minutes (mins), a clear solution was attained to which 1 mL of hydrogen peroxide was added dropwise. A change in colour was noticed to pale golden-yellow and later to dark brown hue within 30 mins of stirring which is due to the oxidation of Ce(III) to Ce(IV) [32]. Subsequently, the rapid addition of 3 mL ammonia solution led to the precipitation of crimson-colored loosely aggregated particles. The solution was then heated in a water bath at 60 ⁰C for 45 mins to observe the solution change its color to yolk yellow. The precipitate was then filtered, redispersed in 20 mL DI water by maintaining an acidic pH (pH = 1) with the addition of 1:5 v/v aqueous nitric acid before heating to reflux. When the color of the solution changed to yellowish green, 0.1 g urea and 0.2 g PVA were dissolved in 2 mL water separately and were added dropwise with continued stirring for 45 mins at 75 ⁰C. The temperature was further raised to 100 ⁰C and stirred for another 90 mins to obtain the QDs. Generally, higher temperature is required for the synthesis of QDs. With the addition of H2O2 in this synthesis route, QDs could be synthesized even at lower temperatures. The solution was filtered and washed several times with water and ethanol, after which the collected precipitate was dried overnight in a hot-air oven at 60 ⁰C for further use [31]. Pristine CeO2 quantum dots were prepared by the same procedure using 1 M cerium nitrate without the addition of the dopant, gadolinium sulphate.

The QY of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was estimated using the comparative method [33]. Tryptophan in water (neutral pH) at 25 ⁰C having a QY (std) of 0.14 was used as the standard solution. The refractive index (η) of water is 1.33 as it was taken as the solvent medium for preparing both solutions.

where, QY, I and A represent fluorescence quantum yield, integrated emission intensity and absorbance maxima for Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs respectively.

In order to examine the structural properties, the synthesized materials were characterized by recording PXRD. The obtained PXRD pattern in Fig. 1 A of bare CeO2 QDs had an accurate resemblance to the JCPDS card No. 00-043-1002 owing to the cubic fluorite structure of CeO2 with a space group of Fm-3m (255) [34]. The characteristic diffraction peaks at 27.8⁰, 32.0⁰, 46.9⁰, 55.9⁰, 58.9⁰, 69.3⁰, 76.5⁰, 79⁰ and 88.3⁰ correspond to the plane (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331), (420) and (422) respectively of CeO2 QDs. Even after doping 0.1 M Gd ions into the crystal lattice of CeO2 QDs, no additional peaks were spotted, which implies that the structure has not been completely deformed. But a slight shift towards higher angle along with the broadening of the characteristic peaks were noted and compared to the bare CeO2 QDs. The peak at 27.8⁰ corresponding to the (111) plane of undoped CeO2 QDs shifted to 28.14⁰, 28.28⁰, 28.43⁰, and 28.58⁰ for 2.5 %, 5 %, 7.5 % and 10 % doping of Gd ions into CeO2 QDs which has been represented through the narrowed XRD spectra in Fig. S1. This confirms that Gd ions which are smaller in size than cerium ions have been successfully doped into the interstitial positions of the cubic structure of CeO2 QDs. Further, the metal oxygen bond can be recognized from FT-IR technique (Fig. S2). A prominent peak at 483.0 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration modes of O-Ce-O in undoped CeO2 QDs, which shifted to 496.4 cm−1 in the case of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs possibly due to Gd doping [35] .

From Fig. 1B, it could be observed that the powder CeO2 QDs exhibit absorption peaks in the UV region (200-400 nm), thus giving a pale-yellow color to the material. Ce3+ and Ce4+ exhibit absorption peaks around 230-280 and 300-400 nm respectively in the UV-Vis spectra. The absorption peak at 263 and 370 nm, confirms the presence of both Ce3+ and Ce4+ respectively [36–38]. Due to the charge transfer from the O 2p subshell to Ce 4f subshell, an absorption maximum arises at 301 nm which is the distinctive peak of CeO2 nanoparticle [39].

The fluorescence behaviour of the undoped CeO2 QDs was probed by exciting 1 mg/mL of the powder sample at a wide range of wavelengths in the UV region as elucidated in Fig. 1C. On the relaxation of the excited cerium ions to the ground state, Ce3+ ions could emit fluorescence signal. Upon optimizing, the maximum emission was recorded at a wavelength of 355 nm when excitation was performed at 255 nm. Gradually, all the synthesized materials were excited at 255 nm and their fluorescence intensities were compared as illustrated in Fig. 1D. The maximum intensity was exhibited by Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs and thus it was adopted as the optimized ratio for doping Gd in CeO2 to develop sensor probe. Further, various concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 2.5 mg/mL were employed to fix an appropriate concentration of the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs in DI water as a dispersing medium for further sensor design and application. From Fig. S3 it could be noticed that 1.5 mg/mL of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs dispersed in DI water exhibited maximum intensity and thus it was chosen as the optimum concentration for the fluorescence sensor design. The increment in the fluorescence emission intensity is due to the increasing concentration of quantum dots. By choosing an appropriate dopant for the host material and controlling the percentage of doping, the emission wavelength could be tuned prudently, making the sensor design suitable for multicolor imaging [40]. The QY of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was calculated to be 49.53 %. The effect of pH on the fluorescence behaviour of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was tested and is presented as Fig. S4 (A). The fluorescence intensities were recorded in the pH range from 2 to 12 and it could be observed that the response was almost similar in the pH range from 2 to 8, after which it is found to decrease. Thus, Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs exhibit very good stability in the pH range from 2 to 8 and we have chosen the neutral pH (7.0) as the optimum pH for all further investigations. The photostability of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was investigated by continuously irradiating UV light of 265 nm for 72 h and analyzing the change in fluorescence intensity at regular intervals of 1 h for the first 12 h and then at an interval of 12 h. The obtained results are portrayed in Fig. S4 (B) and a drop of 3.3 % in the intensity was noticed after 3 days of analysis, indicating that the Gd-doped CeO2 QDs showed good stability against photobleaching.

The morphological characterization of CeO2 QDs was carried out using HRTEM and illustrated in Fig. 2 A and 2D which shows that the synthesized QDs are spherical. Further, the successful formation of QDs was validated through Fig. 2B, since the average size of the prepared QDs was calculated to be 3.1 nm. The amorphous rings obtained from the SAED pattern in Fig. 2C match well with the PXRD pattern. The d-spacing from Fig. 2F was found to be around 0.19 nm corresponding to the (220) plane of CeO2 QDs. After doping with 0.075 M Gd, it could be noticed that there was no observable change in the size of the spherically shaped QDs from Fig. 3 A and 3B, in comparison with the parent material. The SAED pattern in Fig. 3C was found to be compatible with the diffraction patterns in PXRD. The d-spacing calculated from Fig. 3D and 3F of 0.32 nm and 0.27 nm corresponds to (111) and (200) planes respectively of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs. The elemental mapping represented in Fig. S5 confirmed that the dopant amount was in line with the expectation and all the elements Ce, Gd and O were present in the sample.

Raman spectroscopy was carried out to obtain information about the defects created on the crystal lattice due to the added dopants and Ov by comparing bare CeO2 QDs against Gd-doped CeO2 QDs as depicted in Fig. 4 A. On assessing the Raman spectra of bare CeO2 QDs, the weak peak at 269.95 cm−1 corresponds to the second order transverse acoustic (2TA) and the weak peak at 597.12 cm−1 is attributed to the defect induced vibration (D) band which is the Ov raised by compensating the charge that appeared after the conversion of Ce4+ to Ce3+ while maintaining electro neutrality. The peak at 808.9 cm−1 is attributed to having originated from O2− replacement of cerium (IV) atoms by cerium (III) atoms or impurity atoms [41,42]. The first-order Raman peak (f2g) at 457.9 cm−1 has been enlarged and represented in Fig. 4B, which is the characteristic peak from the Ce-O stretching vibration due to the symmetric shaking of 8 oxygen atoms around each cerium cation with fluorite structure [43]. The second-order Raman scattering peak (2LO) is located at 1046.30 cm−1. Upon introduction of the Gd ions as dopant and also with continuous increase in the concentration of Gd dopant, a shift in the f2g peak could be observed which has been illustrated in Fig. 4B. A red shift was observed from 457.9 cm−1 to 451.3 cm−1 for 0 % to 7.5 % Gd doping indicates a minor deformation in the crystal lattice of CeO2 QDs which have been caused by structural defects (inset to Fig. 4B). However, upon Gd doping of 10 %, a blue shift to 455.37 cm−1 was observed, which could be due to the contraction or reallocation of the lattice due to the excess amount of the dopant (higher than the optimal level). The concentration of Ce3+ ions and Ov are crucial for enhancing the catalytic activity. I600/I450 value reflects the degree of the defect sites on the nanostructure, including the relative content of the Ov. The creation of Ov could be due to the MO8 type complex defects and these are created when Ce4+ is reduced to Ce3+ or substituted by Gd3+. An increase in the Ov was found for doped Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs, which indicates that Gd ions were incorporated into the CeO2 phase and thus led to an increase in the degree of defects [44].

XPS technique was carried out to understand the elemental composition and electronic states in Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs. The survey spectrum depicted in Fig. 4C clarifies that Ce, O, and Gd elements are present in the material in the synthesized QD. The high-resolution spectra were further explored and from the deconvoluted spectrum of Ce 3d represented in Fig. 4D, the mixed valence state (+3 and +4) for Ce ions could be confirmed [45]. The peaks centered at 889.6 eV, 884.4 eV, 886.85 eV and 881.8 eV are conferred to the Ce 3d5/2 state of Ce4+ and Ce3+ ions respectively. Similarly, the peaks at 907.25 eV, 900.4 eV, 903.5 eV, and 897.9 eV are attributed to the Ce 3d3/2 state of Ce4+ and Ce3+ ions [46,47]. The high-resolution spectrum for O 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks as displayed in Fig. 4E. The peaks at 528.9 eV, 531.15 eV and 533.45 eV correspond to Ce-O-Ce, Ce-O-OH and O-OH bonding respectively [48]. There was no observable peak in the PXRD spectrum corresponding to the oxides or hydroxides of gadolinium, which infers that Gd remains as a dopant (could be due to the addition of a very low amount of Gd) incorporated in the CeO2 lattice and there is no secondary phase formation. The core level spectra of Gd 4d are revealed in Fig. 4F with two peaks at 142.8 eV and 146.15 eV owing to Gd 4d5/2 and Gd 4d3/2 respectively. The peak at 149.3 eV is due to Si 2s and is due to the silica support employed for XPS measurement and the peak at 284.2 eV is indicative of the carbon signal, which is commonly used as a reference for calibrating the binding energy scale in XPS. [49,50].

We further envisioned to investigate the sensing behavior of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs sensor by adding 200 µL of various solutions of metal ions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Li+, Cu2+, Co2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+) with a uniform concentration of 10 mM and then screening the quenching efficiency towards each metal ion. It could be observed from Fig. S6 (A and B) that even though each of the samples could slightly quench the fluorescence of the sensor. However, almost absolute quenching could be observed only when Fe3+ was added, which demonstrates the high selectivity of the probe toward Fe3+. Fe3+ has a high electron-accepting affinity which facilitates the fast electron transfer between the electron-deficient Fe3+ and electron-rich probe [51] and this could be accounted for the selectivity of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs towards the detection of Fe3+. A similar trend was observed for the CeO2 QDs based sensor but with a higher degree of interference.

Since both CeO2 QDs and Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs exhibited selective quenching towards Fe3+, the sensing performance of these probes towards Fe3+ was assessed at room temperature by injecting different concentrations of Fe3+ ions into the sensing platforms as shown in Fig. 5 A and 5C. The quenching process could be observed visually on the addition of various concentrations (from lower to higher) of Fe3+ to Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs in a UV light chamber (Fig. S7). Further, fluorescence emission intensities were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 255 nm. Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of quenched fluorescence ratio F0/F was plotted against Fe3+ concentration is shown in Fig. 5B and 5D for CeO2 and Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs, respectively, where F0 represents emission intensity of the sensor and F denotes the quenched fluorescence intensity. The quenching constants found from the SV plots were 0.780 nM−1 and 1.321 fM−1 for CeO2 and Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs, respectively. Interestingly, the linear range for the detection of Fe3+ using Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was observed to be 0.13 to 3.6 fM and from 3.6 to 6.0 fM with LOD of 0.039 fM. However, the CeO2 QDs have displayed a relatively lesser performance towards the detection of Fe3+ with two linear ranges from 0.6 to 6 nM and 6 nM to 8 nM with LOD of 0.06 nM. The sensing performance of the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs is found to be better than when compared with other reported QDs-based Fe3+ sensors charted in Table 1 .

To further examine the efficacy of the sensor towards the detection of Fe3+ in the presence of other interferents, selectivity studies were carried out and the results are presented in Fig. 6 A and 6B. During analysis, 200 µL of 10 mM of each interferent was added to the cuvette containing a mixture of 1.5 mL of the probe and 200 µL of 1 mM Fe3+. As observed from Fig. 6A, the CeO2 QDs exhibit reasonably good selectivity towards Fe3+ sensing in the presence of most of the metal ions, whereas a significant interference from Fe2+ was noticed. Noticeably, in the case of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs as probe (Fig. 6B), either no sign or negligible interference was noticed even in the presence of 10 times higher concentration of the interfering species and this witnessed the impressive selectivity of the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs probe.

Further, to check the sensor's reusability property, the fluorescence intensity spectrum of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was measured initially which was further quenched by the addition of 10 mM Fe3+ and then thoroughly washed the system to free the adsorbed analyte from the probe. Further, the probe was dried and its fluorescence intensity was rechecked for 5 times. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of the probe did not change even after 5 measurements (blue curve), which confirms the reusability of the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs for fluorescence sensing.

The feasibility of the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs sensor in detecting Fe3+ in real water samples was tested. Known concentrations of Fe3+ were spiked into tap and drinking water samples. These samples were detected utilizing the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs sensor developed and their fluorescence emission spectra were measured. This experiment was conducted thrice and the obtained results are presented in Table 2 . A very good recovery ranging from 92 to 100 % was found and therefore the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs based probe could be successfully extended towards the detection of Fe3+ ions in complex environmental or biological samples.

Static and dynamic quenching are the two types of fluorescence quenching processes that occur due to the formation of non-fluorescent complexes and electron transfer respectively. Detailed experiments were carried out to investigate the quenching mechanism [60]. At first, the quenching constants were obtained using the SV plot shown in Fig. 5B and 5D in order to study the interaction between Fe3+ and the QDs [61]. The SV plot has shown a linear trend up to a certain concentration after which the linear trend disappeared and this suggested the presence of both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms.

In order to distinguish between the two possible mechanisms, fluorescence lifetime studies were carried out and the results are depicted in Fig. 7 A. This indeed shed light on the decay behavior of the sensor and the changes occurring upon the addition of Fe3+. The average lifetime of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was found to be 0.681 ns; due to the radiative relaxation routine from the excited state to the ground state. A significant decline in fluorescence lifetime to 0.462 ns was observed on adding Fe3+ due to the transfer of charge instigating the excitons to decay faster, thus confirming the mechanism to be dynamic [62]. Further, to explore the mechanism in detail, the exact category of dynamic quenching has to be identified [63]. For this, the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, Fe3+ were compared with the excitation and emission spectra of the donor, Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs as in Fig. 7B, which reveals the overlapping between them. This overlap projects the absorption of emitted energy by the half-filled 3d-orbital of Fe3+ which facilitates non-radiative electron/hole recombination, eventually leading to fluorescence quenching [64]. In order to further validate the quenching mechanism, zeta potential curves were obtained for the Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+. By analyzing Fig. S9, the zeta potential value was found to be 9.5 mV for Gd-CeO2 QDs in the absence of Fe3+, which increased to 17.0 mV in the presence of the analyte and this substantial increase demonstrate the interaction between the probe and the analyte. Finally, Forster distance was calculated and found to be 8.242 Å and the distance between Fe3+ and Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs was calculated to be 9.339 Å which is in the range of 0-100 Å. The fashion of overlapping and the calculated distance infer that the quenching of fluorescence intensity of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs in the presence of Fe3+ occurs through the FRET mechanism [65].

To summarize, CeO2 and Gd-doped CeO2 QDs of different molar ratios were successfully prepared using sol-gel technique and their structural, morphological, and elemental compositions were thoroughly investigated. Their fluorescence abilities were compared and owing to their highest fluorescence intensity, Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs were selected as the fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+. Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs have demonstrated excellent selectivity towards Fe3+ sensing due to the ability of Fe3+ to quench the fluorescence emission. The linear detection range for Fe3+ determination was found to be from 0.13 fM to 3.6 fM and 3.6 fM to 6.0 fM with a LOD of 0.039 fM. The Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs based probe has displayed excellent recoveries during the real sample analysis. The quenching mechanism was thoroughly examined and the change in the fluorescence lifetime of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs on the addition of Fe3+ proved to be dynamic. There was an involvement of the FRET mechanism which was confirmed from the overlapping of the absorption spectra of Fe3+ with the emission spectra of Gd(0.075)Ce(0.925)Ox QDs and also the distance was calculated to be 9.339 Å between the sensor and Fe3+. Doping of Gd on CeO2 not only enhances the fluorescence intensity of the probe, but also results in enhanced selectivity. Meanwhile, the excitation wavelength chosen for this investigation is 255 nm and this requires higher energy. This could be overcome by appropriate choice of dopant material-metal oxide combination which would exhibit excitation in the near IR region. Furthermore, this work opens avenues for designing high performance fluorescence sensors by modulating their properties through various modification strategies such as doping, creating defects, and tuning size and shapes.

Bebeto G Nair: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Kathavarayan Thenmozhi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.
